title: 2 1 Extreme Values
body:
TOPIC 2 : SCALAR FIELDS
                           2.1 EXTREME VALUES

                                  Second Derivative Test
            If f and all its first and second partial derivatives are continuous
              in the neighbourhood of (a, b) and fx (a, b) = fy (a, b) = 0 then
                                                                  2
(i) f has a local maximum at (a, b) if fxx < 0 and D = fxx fyy − fxy > 0 at (a, b).
                                                                   2
(ii) f has a local minimum at (a, b) if fxx > 0 and D = fxx fyy − fxy > 0 at (a, b).
                                                       2
(iii) f has a saddle point at (a, b) if D = fxx fyy − fxy < 0 at (a, b).
                       2
(iv) If D = fxx fyy − fxy = 0 at (a, b) the second derivative test is inconclusive.




A scalar field is a function f (x, y) that assigns every point in space a scalar (number)
value. An example would be the surface temperature at every point in Australia. An
important application of multivariate calculus is the calculation and identification of ex-
treme values of scalar fields — that is, local maxima and minima of scalar fields. With
some minor modifications, we can extend the tools of single-variable calculus to find ex-
treme values of f (x, y).

          Geometrical description of extrema for z = f (x, y)




                                            1
 Local maxima and minima for z = f (x, y) are still calculated by setting the first derivative
equal to zero. But we now have more than one first derivative, so we have to set both
fx = 0 and fy = 0. This will give two equations that we need to solve to generate several
points (x, y). These are then classified using the tests above. Note that a saddle point is
just a 3-D version of a point of inflection.

Finding and classifying extrema in functions of more than one variable can involve some
complicated algebra and you need to take care not to lose solutions along the way. In
particular remember to never divide both sides of an equation by anything that
could be zero!


                                             2

                                     f (x, y) = −x2 − y 2 − 6x + 4y + 5

Example 3 Aviation fuel is a volatile mix of pure kerosene combined with anti-freeze
and naphtha. The flight time from Sydney to Los Angeles is found to be

                         T (a, n) = 0.005a2 + 0.005n2 − 0.03a − 0.05n + 14

where a and n are the number of milligrams of anti-freeze and naphtha (respectively) per
litre of fuel burned.

  1. What would the flight time be if no anti-freeze or naphtha is added to the fuel?

  2. What would be the flight time if the fuel contains 2mg/L anti-freeze and 4mg/L
     naphtha?

  3. How much anti-freeze and naphtha should be added to the fuel in order to minimise
     the flight time and what is the minimum flight time?




                                                          6
